steps.text: Collect samples from stationary sources using the OTM-50 method.
Use passivated silicon ceramic lined stainless-steel canisters for sample collection.
Ensure sample gas temperatures are <250 °F and moisture is <3% volume/volume (v/v).
Conduct sample conditioning for moisture, acid gas, and temperature management if criteria are not met.
Train source sampling teams in EPA test methods 1, 2, 3, and 4.
Train laboratory analysis teams in the use of GC/MS as described in EPA compendium Method TO-15A and ASTM D5466-21.
Select GC columns, GC conditions, and MS conditions as needed for analysis.
Modify sample preparation portions of the method to overcome interferences.
Configure the VFC Canister Sampling System with Water/Acid Gas Management.
Soak glassware in hot soapy water at 50 °C or higher.
Rinse glassware three times with hot tap water.
Rinse glassware three times with DI water.
Rinse glassware three times with methanol.
Bake glassware in a vacuum oven at 65 – 100 °C with vacuum for a minimum of 2 hours.
Rinse impingers with DI water and refill between runs.
Soak coated stainless-steel fittings in methanol for at least five minutes and repeat three times.
Soak stainless-steel fittings in water for at least five minutes and repeat three times.
Bake coated stainless-steel fittings in a vacuum oven at 65 – 100 °C with vacuum for a minimum of two hours.
Heat PFA sample lines to maximum operating temperature and flush with nitrogen or zero air for approximately two hours.
Rinse highly contaminated sample lines with methanol then heat and flush as required.
Determine appropriate sampling system configuration.
Determine stack gas moisture content.
Determine if acid gases are suspected or are present based on process knowledge.
Use the VFC Sampling System with Water/Acid Gas Management for gases where the moisture is >3% v/v and/or acid gases are present.
Use the Direct VFC Sampling System for gases where the moisture is confirmed to be ≤3% v/v and acid gases are not suspected.
Use the VFC Canister Sampling System with Multiple Canisters for emissions streams with unknown concentrations or VFC concentrations above the analysis calibration range.
Prepare the VFC Canister Sampling System with Water/Acid-Gas Management impinger conditioning system.
Prepare the Direct VFC Sampling System.
Connect the sample probe liner to the heated glass filter housing.
Connect the critical orifice assembly to the bypass line.
Heat the sampling train once the sample extraction system assembly is complete.
Operate the bypass pump at a flow rate of approximately 1 Lpm.
Collect the source emissions sample.
Once the canisters have been sampled, place them in their individual protective containers and then in a shipping box.
Store canisters in a dry location at ambient laboratory temperature.
Ship canisters to laboratory for analysis.
Upon receipt of the canisters at the laboratory, measure and record the canister pressure.
Compare the final pressure and associated sample volume with the theoretical volume sampled in the field from the sample time and orifice flow rate provided on the COC.
Samples must be analyzed within 30 days.
Determine the sampling system background.
Setup, heat, and leak check the sampling system as described in Sections 8.5 and 8.6.
Connect and deliver a flow of UHP nitrogen or zero air to the inlet of the sample probe at a flow rate slightly exceeding that of the sampling system bypass flow rate.
Purge the system for at least two minutes.
Collect the sample as described in Section 8.6.
Collect a 4 to 5 L sample in a 6 L canister.
Submit the canister for analysis as a sample.
Check the field integrity of collected samples.
Perform the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 8.6.2.8.
Flag data that differ between the two approaches by ≥20% RPD and indicate if leakage is suspected and invalidate the sample with a similar explanation.
Provide COC information.
Perform gas dilution system evaluations by generating and analyzing blank samples using humidified zero air.
Perform mass flow or pressure gauge audits on a yearly basis.
Recalibrate dynamic gas dilution systems once per year using NIST traceable primary flow standards.
Recalibrate static gas dilution systems once per year using a NIST traceable primary pressure gauge.
Perform maintenance on static gas dilution systems to have calibrated pressure transducer(s).
Maintain records of calibration results.
Leak check GC/MS systems by monitoring the mass spectrometer vacuum or performing an air/water background check prior to sample analysis.
Demonstrate initial capability (IDC) by performing required actions.
Perform initial calibration (ICAL) with a multipoint curve.
Evacuate clean passivated stainless-steel canisters, or equivalent, to 50 mTorr then inject 50 µL of DI water using a 100-microliter glass displacement syringe.

Prepare VFC calibration standards by diluting individual neat standards and/or from vendor certified pressurized cylinders containing a mixture of the VFC standard targets in nitrogen. Vendor certified standard gasses must be accurate to 5%.

Determine the calibration concentration range needed to span the measured sample concentrations. An example calibration range is approximately 0.5, 2.5, 5.0, 10.0, and 20.0 ppbv for each compound except CF4, which may be calibrated using 10-fold higher standard concentrations due lower injection volumes needed to avoid breakthrough.
Use individual canister calibration standards and the same injection volume to generate calibration curves.
Use either a dynamic or static gas dilution system, or equivalent technique, to blend the VFC calibration gas with zero air.
After preparation, allow the pressurized calibration standard canisters to equilibrate at ambient laboratory temperature for a minimum of 12 hours prior to instrumental analysis.
Calibration gases may be generated by dynamic dilution of a certified compressed gas cylinder containing VFC targets in nitrogen.
After allowing the nominal standard mixture to flow and equilibrate with humidified zero air for a minimum of 30 minutes in the mixing chamber, sample the gas standard mixture with a canister.
The final pressure of the calibration standard canister must not exceed the maximum pressure permitted by the GC/MS pre-concentrator unit.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 7 in Section 12 of this method.
Connect a pressure transducer or gauge to a clean evacuated canister to monitor the canister pressure as gases are added.
Add the VFC calibration standard and the zero air diluent gases separately through a manifold or by direct connection of the gas to the standard canister or vessel.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 8 in Section 12 of this method.

Tune/optimize the MS to demonstrate acceptable performance across the selected ion mass range according to the manufacturer’s specifications upon initial installation of the instrument and following significant preventive maintenance or repair activities.
Verify the MS tune according to the manufacturer’s specifications prior to initial calibration.
Use optional tune verification using bromofluorobenzene (BFB).
Add the IS through a dedicated non-sample port in the pre-concentrator at the same concentration for each injection.
Choose the quantitation ion for each IS compound as the most abundant ion (base peak) unless there is a spectral interference.
Flag samples in which IS area response differs by more than 40% from the mean IS area response and/or in which the IS retention times differ by more than ±5 seconds of the average RT.
Condition pre-concentrator focusing traps as specified by the manufacturer when first installed.
Perform a leak check of the canister lines on the pre-concentrator system prior to opening the canister valves.
Analyze an LB to verify the pre-concentrator system meets the method criteria.
Perform ICAL following the procedures in this section.
Prepare the VFC calibration standards using procedures in Sections 10.1-10.3.
Prepare the analytical sequence for instrument calibration.
Run one LB prior to the calibration standards.
Receive canister samples from the field and cross check COC forms with canister identification numbers. Resolve any disparities before analyzing canisters.
Measure and record the vacuum from field samples. The sample canisters should measure 17 kPa (5 in. Hg) or greater vacuum.

Calculate the volume sampled in the field from the laboratory measured pressure and compare with the field sampled volume. Flag data that differs between the two approaches by more than >5% RPD and indicate leakage is suspected or invalidate the data with a similar explanation.

Canisters may be pressurized for analysis with zero air, research plus nitrogen, or UHP nitrogen. Minimum sample pressures will depend on the size of the canister and the capability of the pre-concentrator to remove the desired aliquot of the sample.
Measure and record the final canister pressure. Calculate the dilution factor resulting from sample pressurization.
Allow each pressurized canister to sit for a minimum of 12 hours to equilibrate before analysis. Canisters have a maximum hold time of 30 days prior to analysis.
Organize and load the canister samples for instrumental analysis.

Record sample names or identifiers, canister pressure, sample description, and total milliliters (mL) used for analysis in laboratory notebooks. Field samples must be analyzed using the same injection volume used for calibration standards.
Connect samples to instrument sampler/autosampler and check for leaks.

If the system is leak free, open the canister valves prior to starting the analytical sequence. If a leak is detected, re-tighten the connection and perform another leak check. Do not open the canister valve if the leak check fails.

Generate an analytical sequence and sample batch that at a minimum includes the following samples: One LB at the beginning of the sequence, a daily CCV after the LB, field samples, including one duplicate analysis of a field sample.
Run the sample sequence.
At the conclusion of data acquisition, use the same software settings established during the calibration procedure to qualitatively identify analyte peaks in the predetermined retention time windows.
Confirm the identity of each analyte by comparing its retention time with that of the corresponding target analyte peak in an ICAL standard or CCV.
Confirm that the ions are consistent with the continuing calibration results. The signals for all characteristic masses shall be present and reach a maximum within the same two consecutive scans.
The retention time difference between the target compound in the sample and the CCV shall agree within ±6 scans or ±6 seconds of this difference in the CCV standard.
After the entire sequence has been analyzed on the instrument, review the analytical results as described in Section 9 for completeness and performance requirements prior to data reporting.
Reanalyze samples that failed performance criteria. Dilute samples as necessary to measure target analytes within the calibration range.
Record and report deviations to performance criteria and actions to report compliant data.
Measure the pressure of each canister upon receipt and compare the volume collected as received to the field post-test volume.
Flag the sample for potential exclusion from the data set if the volume as received is >5% RPD.
Check for leaks, perform maintenance, and reanalyze.
Prepare fresh standards and reanalyze ICAL.
Flag any target analytes that are manually integrated in the calibration curve.
Check for instrument leaks and reanalyze.
Check instrument for contamination and reanalyze.
Reprepare the MDL Confirmation standard.
Reanalyze the MDL study and repeat confirmation.
Check for leaks in the analytical system, repair and reanalyze.
Check for leaks in the standard preparation system.
Check for instrument leaks and reanalyze.
Prepare fresh standard and reanalyze.
Check for leaks in the chromatographic system, repair and reanalyze.
Flag any sample data that does not meet IS retention time criteria.
Verify that the primary quantitation ion is detected at 3 times signal to noise and the retention time is within ±5 seconds of the initial calibration standard.
Retune and recalibrate instrument and reanalyze.
Flag any sample results with failing IS responses.
Prepare new CCV at least every 30 days.
Reanalyze all samples after the last passing CCV.
Re-prep and reanalyze CCV.
Perform instrument maintenance and reanalyze.
Clean the MS source and rerun the ICAL.
Flag target analytes that do not meet ending CCV criteria.
Analyze one or more cleaned canister as an LB from a given batch of cleaned canisters.
Analyze other cans in batch as LBs.
Check canister cleaning system for contamination.
Reclean failed canisters.
Verify that the amount of CO2 present in the samples does not bias the analytical results.
Optimize the analytical system to reduce/eliminate bias, recalibrate, and reanalyze.
Flag any sample results generated using a system that exceeds the ±30 percent bias threshold.

preconditions.text: If moisture is >3% volume/volume (v/v), conduct sample conditioning.
If sample gas temperatures are >=250 °F, conduct sample conditioning.
If acid gases are present, conduct sample conditioning.
If the source is a combustion source, the moisture content is assumed to be >3% v/v.
If the conditions listed in Section 8.1 for direct sampling are met, the canister sample can be collected directly from the source.
Prior to conducting the IDC, the analyst must meet the calibration requirements outlined in Section 10.
If concentrations are >3x MDL, they must be within ±25% of one another.
If sample analytes <3 x MDL or <50% of project-required reporting limit, whichever is higher.

constraints.text: This method does not purport to include all safety issues or procedures needed when collecting canister samples from stationary vents and stacks.
Among other precautions, the user should become familiar with the safety recommendations in the gas analyzer’s manual.
Exercise extreme care in working with high-pressure gas cylinders.
Exposure to suspected carcinogens or other compounds with serious health risks should be avoided in all circumstances.
Minimize dead volume of the system.
Do not use butyl rubber or PFA materials.
Minimize the use of fluoropolymers and fluoroelastomers to avoid adsorption and/or off-gassing of compounds of interest or introduction of other potential interferences.
Connection tubing for the dynamic dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316) or silicon-ceramic coated stainless-steel.
Mixing chambers or manifolds must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic coated stainless-steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
Connection tubing for the static dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316).
Carrier gas lines supplying the GC must be constructed from clean stainless-steel or copper tubing with non-polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) thread sealants.
Flow controllers, cylinder regulators, or other pneumatic components fitted with rubber components are not suitable.
The canister as received should be within 5% RPD of the field volume.
Measured values +/-3% of calculated values based on certified concentrations.
Calibrated against NIST traceable primary flow standards with uncertainties <=0.25%.
Pressure transducer(s) must have an accuracy of ± 0.1 % full scale or 0.13 kPa, whichever is smaller.
Each calibration level must be calculated to be within ±20% of its true value.
% RSD of seven replicate injections must be less than 25%.
Average % recovery of seven replicates must be within ±30% of the concentration prepared in 9.2.3.5.
The lowest calibration concentration must be ≥3x MDL and within ±30% of each target compound spike concentration.
Retention time must be within +/-5 seconds of the most recent calibration.
